The claimant or individual or their attorney or authorized representative may present the claimant’s or individual’s case;
The EPIP administrator may present the EPIP administrator’s case;
The Division may present the Division’s case;
The claimant or individual shall be entitled to a brief rebuttal after the conclusion of the EPIP administrator’s case and/or the Division’s case;
The hearing officer may hear closing arguments in the same order as the presentation of evidence; and
Dispositive motions are prohibited.
Evidence.
The rules of evidence under this chapter shall be under State Government Article, §10-213, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Hearsay, in the form of medical records and certified forms filled out by licensed health care providers, shall be permitted at the hearing.
Additional evidence may not be introduced unless the party seeking to introduce it demonstrates to the satisfaction of the hearing officer that the new evidence:
Is relevant and material;
Was not discovered before the claim was filed; and
Could not have been discovered before the claim was filed with the exercise of due diligence.
All hearings shall be on the record. The record shall include:
All pleadings, motions, responses, correspondence, memoranda, including proposed findings of fact and conclusions of law, and requests filed by the parties;
All hearing notices;
All documentary and other tangible evidence received or considered;
A statement of each fact officially noticed;
All stipulations;
All offers of proof and objections;
All rulings, orders, and decisions, proposed or final;
Matters placed on the record in connection with ex parte communication;
The recording of the hearing, and any pre hearing proceeding, and any transcript of the recording prepared by a court reporting service; and
Any other item required by law.
Interpreters.

If a party or witness cannot readily hear, speak, or understand the spoken or written English language, and applies to the hearing officer in advance of the hearing for the appointment of a qualified interpreter to assist that party or witness, the hearing officer shall appoint a qualified interpreter to provide assistance during the hearing.

With the approval of the hearing officer, a party who intends to offer the testimony of a witness who cannot readily hear, speak, or understand the spoken or written English language, may arrange for a qualified interpreter to assist the witness.
An interpreter shall take an oath or affirm that the interpreter will accurately translate.
Burden of Proof.

The claimant shall bear the burden of proving, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the Division or EPIP administrator erred in making the initial claim decision under dispute, given the evidence available at the time of the initial decision.

The individual who has been disqualified from receiving benefits under Labor and Employment Article §8.3-901, Annotated Code of Maryland shall bear the burden of proving, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the individual should not have been disqualified.
Closed Hearings. Unless otherwise provided by statute, all hearings conducted under this chapter are closed to the public.
Recording.
The proceedings shall be recorded.
The record need not be transcribed unless requested by a party.
The cost of a typewritten transcript of any proceeding or part of a proceeding shall be paid by the party requesting the transcript.

Except as provided under §B(20)(a) of this regulation, cameras, tape recorders, and other electronic and photographic equipment of any type are not permitted at the hearing, unless the equipment is intended to be introduced into evidence or used to present evidence.
Recusal. A hearing officer shall be recused from the review of an appeal and from participating in a hearing if the hearing officer:
Has personal knowledge of the facts which gave rise to the appeal;
Has a personal or business relationship with any of the parties or witnesses; or
For any other reason may be unable to act impartially in the matter.

Decisions. After consideration of the testimony and other evidence the hearing officer shall issue a final written order, including any penalties or fees issued under Labor and Employment Article, §8.3-101 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland, no more than 10 business days after the conclusion of the hearing.
Decision to Employer. Upon receipt of the decision by the Division, the Division shall forward a copy of the decision to the employer.
Judicial Review. A party aggrieved by the final order is entitled to judicial review of the decision under State Government Article, §10-222, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Reconsideration. An employer is entitled to request reconsideration by the Division of a determination of the employer’s contribution liability.
Manner of Filing:
Requests for reconsideration shall be filed within 30 days, unless good cause can be shown, of notice of the contribution liability;
Requests for reconsideration shall be in writing; and
Requests shall include why the requestor believes the contribution liability to be in error.
Reconsideration shall be conducted by Division personnel who did not participate in the contribution liability determination at issue.
A decision on the reconsideration shall be issued within 30 days.
Appeals.
The appeals process is only available to employers who have completed the reconsideration process as described in §A of this regulation.
The appeal shall be filed with the Division within 30 days of the reconsideration denial unless the employer can show good cause.
Absent unusual circumstances, a hearing shall be held on the appeal within 60 days of filing of the appeal.
Hearing Notice. Employers shall be provided with reasonable written notice of a hearing.
The hearing notice shall contain:
The date, time, place, and nature of the hearing;
A statement of the right to present witnesses, documents, and other forms of evidence, and the right to cross-examine witnesses;
A statement of the right to request subpoenas for witnesses and evidence, specifying the costs, if any, associated with the request;
A copy of the hearing procedure;
A statement of the right or restrictions pertaining to representation;
A statement that failure to appear for the scheduled hearing may result in an adverse action against the employer; and
A statement that the employer and Division may agree to the evidence and waive their right to appear at the hearing.

Service of Notices, Orders, and Other Documents. Except as provided by prior agreement, the hearing officer shall serve notices, orders, and other documents to the employer and any other parties in one of the following ways:
Electronically; and
By personal delivery; or
By mailing a copy of the document, first class, postage prepaid, to the employer’s last known business or home address; and
If the employer is represented by counsel, also by delivering or mailing a copy of the document, first class, postage prepaid, to the employer’s attorney.
The hearing officer shall send the hearing notice to the employer:
At least 15 days before the hearing; or
If the employer has agreed to a date for which 15 days notice cannot be given, at the earliest time possible.
Representation.
An employer may appear individually or, if appearance by an attorney or representative is permitted or required by law, through an attorney authorized to practice in Maryland.
Any notice, decision, or other matter required to be sent to an employer may also be sent to the employer’s attorney of record at the attorney’s address.

If an employer is represented by an attorney or appears through an authorized representative, then examination and cross-examination of witnesses, and objections and motions on the employer’s behalf shall be made solely by the attorney or the authorized representative.
Failure to Appear. A hearing may proceed as scheduled in the absence of the employer if the employer has:
Been served in accordance with §B(4) of this regulation; and
Failed to obtain a postponement of the hearing from the hearing officer under these requirements.
Postponement.
The hearing officer may postpone a hearing only if a written request for postponement is filed with the hearing officer not later than 10 days before the date of the hearing.

If a request for postponement is received later than 10 days before the date of the hearing, the hearing officer shall deny the request unless they determine that there was good cause which justified the delay.
Failure to retain counsel or to timely request a subpoena may not be considered good cause under this regulation.
A request for postponement based on failure to obtain service on a witness may not be granted if the employer has failed to comply with the subpoena procedures set forth at COMAR 09.01.02.
Discovery. There is no pre-hearing discovery.
Subpoenas. Subpoena procedures are governed by COMAR 09.01.02.
Conduct of the Proceedings.
The hearing officer may impose reasonable time limitations.

The Maryland Rules of Civil Procedure may be used as a guide in resolving procedural issues governing the conduct of the hearing that are not addressed in this chapter and the Administrative Procedure Act.

The hearing officer may conduct all or any part of the hearing by telephone, video conference, or other electronic means, in accordance with State Government Article, §10-211, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Order of Proceedings. Absent unusual circumstances, the order of proceedings shall be as follows:
Opening statements and preliminary matters may be heard;
All individuals planning to testify shall be sworn before testifying;
The employer or their attorney or authorized representative may present the employer’s case;
The Division may present the Division’s case;
The employer shall be entitled to a brief rebuttal after the conclusion of the Division’s case;
The hearing officer may hear closing arguments in the same order as the presentation of evidence; and
Dispositive motions are prohibited.
Evidence.
The rules of evidence under this chapter shall be under State Government Article, §10-213, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Additional evidence may not be introduced unless the party seeking to introduce it demonstrates to the satisfaction of the hearing officer that the new evidence:
Is relevant and material;
Was not discovered before the contribution liability was determined; and
Could not have been discovered before the contribution liability was determined with the exercise of due diligence.
The record shall include:
All pleadings, motions, responses, correspondence, memoranda, including proposed findings of fact and conclusions of law, and requests filed by the employer and Division;
All hearing notices;
All documentary and other tangible evidence received or considered;
A statement of each fact officially noticed;
All stipulations;
All offers of proof and objections;
All rulings, orders, and decisions, proposed or final;
Matters placed on the record in connection with ex parte communication;
The recording of the hearing, and any pre hearing proceeding, and any transcript of the recording prepared by a court reporting service; and
Any other item required by law.
Interpreters.

If an employer or witness cannot readily hear, speak, or understand the spoken or written English language, and applies to the hearing officer in advance of the hearing for the appointment of a qualified interpreter to assist that employer or witness, the hearing officer shall appoint a qualified interpreter to provide assistance during the hearing.

With the approval of the hearing officer, an employer who intends to offer the testimony of a witness who cannot readily hear, speak, or understand the spoken or written English language, may arrange for a qualified interpreter to assist the witness.
An interpreter shall take an oath or affirm that the interpreter will accurately translate.
Burden of Proof. The employer shall bear the burden of proving, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the employer is entitled to a change in the contribution liability.
Open Hearings. Unless otherwise provided by statute, all hearings conducted under this chapter are open to the public.
Recording.
The proceedings shall be recorded;
The record need not be transcribed unless requested by the employer;
The cost of a typewritten transcript of any proceeding or part of a proceeding shall be paid by the entity requesting the transcript; and

Except as provided under §B(16)(a) of this regulation, cameras, tape recorders, and other electronic and photographic equipment of any type are not permitted at the hearing, unless the equipment is intended to be introduced into evidence or used to present evidence.
Recusal. A hearing officer shall be recused from the review of an appeal and from participating in a hearing if the hearing officer:
Has personal knowledge of the facts which gave rise to the appeal;
Has a personal or business relationship with the employer or witnesses; or
For any other reason may be unable to act impartially in the matter.

Decisions. After consideration of the testimony and other evidence the hearing officer shall issue a final written order, including any penalties or fees issued under Labor and Employment Article, §8.3-101 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland, within 90 days of the conclusion of the hearing.
Judicial Review. An employer aggrieved by the final order is entitled to judicial review of the decision under State Government Article, §10-222, Annotated Code of Maryland.

To the extent the dispute resolution procedures, including timelines and good cause, outlined in this chapter are more restrictive with respect to claimants and recipients than those dispute resolution procedures outlined by the Administration, the Administration’s procedures shall control.

To the extent the dispute resolution procedures, including notice requirements and good cause, outlined in this chapter are less restrictive with respect to employers and carriers than those dispute resolution procedures outlined by the Administration, the Administration’s procedures shall control.

annotations.annotation: Labor and Employment Article, §§8.3-101, 403, and 906, Annotated Code of Maryland
Effective date: March 30, 2026 (53:6 Md. R. 290)

section.heading: Definitions—Applicable to Health Occupations Article, §1-301 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland.

para.text: For the purpose of enforcing Health Occupations Article, §1-301 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Group practice” has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §1-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Health Care Service.
“Health care service” has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §1-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.
“Health care service” includes but is not limited to:
Supplies;
Drugs; and
Items furnished to a patient by a health care entity..
“Members of the group” means health care practitioners:
With an ownership interest in the group practice; or

Who are full-time or part-time contractors or employees during the time they furnish services to patients of the group practice that are furnished through the group and are billed in the name of the group.

“Patient care services” means the total patient care time spent on any task performed by a group practice member that addresses the medical needs of specific patients, regardless of whether they involve direct patient encounters, and including but not limited to time spent:
By a member of the group in consultation with other health care practitioners, whether or not they are members of the group; and
Reviewing laboratory tests, as well as the services of other health care practitioners who do not directly treat patients.

“Personally supervised” means the responsibility of a health care practitioner to exercise on-site supervision or immediately available direction for employees performing referred in-office ancillary services or tests.
“Referring health care practitioner” means a health care practitioner, or group practice, who makes a referral.
“Rental or lease of office space” means payments made for the rental or lease of utilities, use of equipment, and secretarial and support staff.
In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Area of concentration” means a limitation of practice by demonstrable training and competence of a health care practitioner.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Essential” means a health care entity would not be able to exist but for the health care practitioner's beneficial interest in the health care entity.
“Health care entity” has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §1-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Health Care Practitioner.
“Health care practitioner has the meaning stated in Health Occupations Article, §1-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Health care practitioner” does not include a mortician, an assisted living manager, or a nursing home administrator, unless the mortician, assisted living manager, or nursing home administrator is licensed, certified, or otherwise authorized under the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, to provide health care services by any board other than the Board of Morticians and Funeral Directors or the Board of Long-Term Care Administrators.
“Maryland Health Care Commission (MHCC)” means the Maryland Health Care Commission.
“Referral” has the meaning stated in Health Occupation Article, §1-301, Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Requestor” means an individual other than the health care practitioner who is an attorney admitted to the Maryland Bar or specially admitted to practice law in the State under Maryland Rules Title 19.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of the Maryland Department of Health or their designee.
“Specialty” means “area of concentration” as defined in §B(1) of this regulation.
A health care practitioner who has or wishes to have a beneficial interest in a health care entity may request from the Secretary an exemption from the self-referral laws.

In order for a health care practitioner to obtain an exemption by the Secretary from the self-referral laws, the health care practitioner shall make a request to the Secretary in the manner specified by the Department that:

Identifies the street address, political subdivision, and area of concentration of both the health care practitioner and the entity in which the practitioner has or wishes to have a beneficial interest;
Demonstrates to the Secretary's satisfaction that the health care practitioner's beneficial interest is:
Essential to finance and to provide the health care entity; and
Needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity;
States when the services are proposed to begin;
Provides the health care practitioner’s:
Name;
Licensing board;
License number;
License expiration date;
Business address; and
Business telephone number;

Includes an attestation in writing that, if the health care practitioner seeks renewal of an exemption from the Secretary and the Secretary no longer deems the beneficial interest to be essential, the practitioner shall:
Immediately cease accepting any referrals from sources prohibited under Health Occupations Article, §1-301 et seq., Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Divest the practitioner's beneficial interest in the entity within 1 year after the exemption is no longer applicable.
Request to MHCC.

The Secretary shall request, in writing, the advice of the Executive Director of the MHCC concerning whether the beneficial interest is needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity.

The Secretary shall provide, with the request to the Executive Director of the MHCC, a copy of any materials submitted to the Department in accordance with §B of this regulation and any additional items which may be pertinent to the MHCC’s review.
The Secretary shall follow the timetable set forth in Regulation .06 of this chapter.

The Executive Director of the MHCC shall advise the Secretary in accordance with the timetable in Regulation .06 of this chapter as to whether the health care entity is needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity.

If additional information is needed by the Secretary or the Executive Director of MHCC for their determination, the Department may ask the health care practitioner to provide additional information to establish that the practitioner’s beneficial interest is:
Essential to finance and to provide the health care entity; and
Needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity.
The Secretary shall make the required determinations within 90 calendar days following receipt of a health care practitioner's request for exemption.

The Secretary shall request advice from the Executive Director of the MHCC as to whether the health care entity is needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity within 30 calendar days of receipt of the health care practitioner’s request for exemption.

The Executive Director of the MHCC shall advise the Secretary as to whether the health care entity is needed to ensure appropriate access for the community to the services provided at the health care entity within 60 calendar days after receipt of the Secretary's letter.

The term of an exemption shall be in conjunction with a health care practitioner's professional license renewal and shall be renewed at the same time the health care practitioner's professional license is renewed.
The exemption expires at the end of its term, unless the exemption is renewed in accordance with this regulation.
An exempted health care practitioner, on renewal, shall meet all the requirements of this chapter at the time of renewal.

Each health care practitioner who has been exempted by the Secretary from the prohibition on self-referral as an essential health care entity needed for access to health care shall renew the health care practitioner's exemption status in the manner provided by the Department at the time of licensure, certification, or other form of practice renewal authorized under the Health Occupations Article, Annotated Code of Maryland, within the time period specified on the form.
At a minimum, the health care practitioner shall provide, at the time of renewal, to the Department:
A copy of the initial exemption request;
A copy of the Secretary’s approval of the initial exemption;
The items specified in Regulation .05B of this chapter; and
Any additional pertinent information as requested by the Secretary.
Each exempted health care practitioner shall keep the appropriate licensing, certification, or other practice authorizing board apprised of the health care practitioner’s current business address.
On request by the Department, the relevant board or boards shall supply the current business addresses of exempted health care practitioners.
The Department shall mail a notice of the required renewal to the health care practitioner at the current business address supplied by the appropriate board.

If an exempted health care practitioner does not receive notice of renewal, it is the exempted health care practitioner's responsibility to notify the Department so that the Secretary may renew the exemption before the expiration of the current exemption.
Failure to Renew Exemption.
If an exempted health care practitioner fails to renew the exemption, the practitioner is not considered exempted under the Health Occupations Article, §1-301(d)(5), Annotated Code of Maryland.

If an exempted health care practitioner fails to renew the exemption, and fails to carry out the agreed requirements set forth in Regulation .05B(6) of this chapter, the practitioner shall be subject to disciplinary action as set forth in Health Occupations Article, §1-307, Annotated Code of Maryland, for violation of the self-referral laws.

section.heading: Guidelines Governing the Transfer of Patients Between Hospitals.

para.text: If a hospital is able to provide adequate care to a patient, the hospital may not transfer the patient to another hospital unless:
One or more of the following circumstances is present:
The patient does not have a physician with privileges at the hospital and the patient refuses treatment by any physician with privileges at the hospital;
The patient or a legally authorized representative of the patient requests the transfer;
The transfer is in compliance with relevant sections of Health-General Article, Title 10, Subtitle 8, Annotated Code of Maryland;
The transfer is in compliance with the Interstate Compact on Mental Health as adopted in Health-General Article, Title 11, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
The transfer is in compliance with any agreement entered into between the Department and a hospital for the provision of acute inpatient psychiatric care in State hospital conversion beds;
These circumstances are documented in the patient's medical record; and
The provisions of this regulation are met.
A hospital may not use the inability of a patient to pay or the source of payment for a patient as a reason to transfer the patient to another hospital.
Before moving a patient, the transferring hospital shall:
Determine that the patient can be transferred without causing harm to the patient;
Notify the receiving hospital of the transfer; and
Unless otherwise provided for in an agreement with the Department for State hospital conversion beds, obtain from the receiving hospital confirmation that:
The receiving hospital consents to accept the patient; and
The patient meets any criteria that the receiving hospital has established for admission regarding:
The patient's required level of care;
Physician services available; and
Other services necessary to treat the patient.
Explanation of Transfer.

Except as otherwise provided in §C(2),, of this regulation, before moving a patient, the transferring hospital shall provide explanation of the reasons for the transfer and any alternative to the transfer to the patient or to a legally authorized representative of the patient.

If the transferring hospital can document that the necessity to move the patient to the receiving hospital is immediate to protect the health, safety, or welfare of the patient, the transferring hospital may give the explanation required in §C(1), of this regulation, concurrently with the transfer.
In effecting a transfer, the transferring hospital shall provide that:

Medically appropriate life-support measures that a reasonable and prudent physician exercising ordinary care would use are used to stabilize the patient before transfer and sustain the patient during transfer;
Appropriate personnel and equipment that a reasonable and prudent physician exercising ordinary care would use are provided and used in the transfer; and
All records necessary for continuing the patient's care are transferred with the patient.
The Department shall inspect hospitals for compliance with this regulation as provided in Regulations .07 and .08 of this chapter.
Penalties.
The Secretary may impose a penalty not exceeding $1,000 per violation on any hospital that violates this regulation.
In determining the amount of the penalty, the Secretary shall consider:
The hospital's history of previous violations;
The seriousness of the violation;
Whether the hospital demonstrated good faith in attempting to comply with this regulation; and
Whether the violation threatened the health or safety of:
A patient; or
The public.
General. In accordance with this regulation, a hospital shall have in effect a credentialing process.
Scope of Credentialing Process. The credentialing process shall apply to any physician who shall admit or treat patients in the hospital.
Specific Standard — Appointment and Employment Process.
In accordance with this section, a hospital shall establish a formal written process for the appointment or employment of a physician by the hospital.
The term of an appointment may not exceed the term established by the standards of the accreditation organization that accredits the hospital.

The formal written appointment or employment process shall provide for a probationary period that shall be successfully completed before the finalization of the appointment or employment of the physician.
As part of the formal written appointment and employment process, the hospital shall collect, verify, review, and document the following information about the physician:
The physician's education;
The clinical expertise of the physician;
The professional experience of the physician including:
Any board certification or specialty training of the physician;
The internship of the physician; and
The residencies of the physician;
Any license or registration to practice a health occupation ever held by the physician, including:
A license to practice medicine; and
DEA registration;
Whether any license or registration to practice a health occupation ever held by the physician has been:
Suspended;
Revoked;
Voluntarily surrendered or not renewed;
Concerning any hospital where the physician was appointed or employed:
The name of the hospital;
The term of appointment or employment;
Privileges held and any disciplinary action taken on the privileges, including suspension, revocation, limitation, or voluntary surrender;
Concerning the physician's professional liability insurance:
The physician's present carrier;
The physician's current limits of coverage;
The physician's current types of coverage;
Restrictions on the physician's coverage; and
Whether or not the physician has maintained continuous malpractice coverage since first obtaining professional insurance;
Any claim that has been made against the physician in the practice of any health occupation and the status of the claim;
The physician's medical history including the physician's current mental and physical health status;
A complaint or report filed with:
The Board of Physicians or any other state medical discipline agency;
A state medical society;
A state disciplinary body; or
A professional or specialty association.
The formal written process shall provide for the documentation of any action taken by the hospital regarding the appointment or employment of the physician.
Uniform Standard Credentialing Form.
A hospital shall use the uniform standard credentialing form approved by the Department for the initial credentialing of a physician seeking appointment or employment.

Use of the uniform standard credentialing form does not preclude a hospital from requiring additional information, attestations, or supplemental documentation as required by that hospital's credentialing process.
A physician seeking hospital privileges shall submit an updated and complete uniform standard credentialing form at the time of application to each hospital.
Specific Standard — Granting of Delineated Clinical Privileges.
In accordance with this section, a hospital shall establish a formal written process for the granting of delineated clinical privileges.
The formal written process shall include:
Criteria for determining whether a physician shall be granted privileges by the hospital to provide specific services;
Criteria for ongoing evaluation of the performance of the services for which privileges have been granted;
Procedures for altering, suspending, or revoking the delineated privileges.
The formal written process shall provide for documentation of any actions taken regarding delineated privileges.
Specific Standard — Reappointment.
In accordance with this section, a hospital shall establish a formal written process for the reappointment of a physician who has been appointed to the hospital.
The term of reappointment shall be 2 years or less.
As part of the formal written appointment process, a hospital shall collect, verify, review, and document the following information about the physician:
An update of the information regarding appointment under §C of this regulation;
Concerning the physician's pattern of performance based on an analysis of the following:
Claims filed against the physician;
Utilization, quality and risk data;
A review of clinical skills;
Adherence to hospital bylaws, policies, and procedures;
Compliance with continuing medical education requirements;
An assessment of current mental and physical health status;
Attitudes, cooperation, and the ability to work with others; and
The results of the Practitioner Performance Evaluation process as described in Health-General Article, §§19-3B-01—19-3B-09, Annotated Code of Maryland.
Specific Standard — Record Maintenance.
In accordance with this section, a hospital shall maintain a separate credentialing file for each physician.
The credentialing file for each physician shall contain documentation relating to the credentialing process required under this regulation.
Disaster Privileges.

During an emergency or disaster in which the hospital's disaster or emergency management plan has been activated, when the Governor has declared that a state of emergency exists, or when the Secretary has issued an order pursuant to Health-General Article, §18-905, Annotated Code of Maryland, the chief executive officer, medical staff president, or designee may grant temporary disaster privileges to licensed physicians who have not been appointed to the hospital's medical staff.
The hospital shall develop a medical staff plan for the granting of disaster privileges that identifies:
The individual responsible for granting disaster privileges;
The responsibilities of that individual;
A system to manage, assign, and supervise the physicians who have been granted disaster privileges; and
The process by which credentials and privileges are verified as soon as the situation allows, ensuring that the process complies with §C of this regulation.
Physicians granted disaster privileges by a hospital shall:
Be registered and trained by the Department as part of the Department's Maryland Physician Volunteer Corps and possess the Department issued photo identification; or
Comply with the hospital's medical staff plan for granting privileges in a disaster, which shall require at least one of the following:
Presentation of a current Maryland license to practice medicine and a valid identification picture (ID) issued by a state, federal, or regulatory agency;

Presentation of a license to practice medicine from another state if a state of emergency has been declared by the Governor and the assistance of the physician has been requested by Maryland pursuant to the Emergency Management Assistance Compact, Public Safety Article, §14-702, Annotated Code of Maryland;
Presentation of a current photo identification card from another Maryland hospital where the physician is a member of the medical staff; or
Verification by a current member of the hospital's medical staff who has personal knowledge regarding the practitioner's identity and current Maryland medical licensure.

Disaster privileges shall be discontinued when the hospital's chief executive officer, medical staff president, or designee determines that the emergency condition no longer exists and that the hospital has adequate resources to meet the patient's needs.
The hospital shall maintain records that include:
The number of hours worked by each physician;
The type of service provided by each physician;
The location where these services were provided; and
Any additional information required by the Department for federal and State reimbursement.

Telemedicine. Notwithstanding any other provision of COMAR 10.07.01.24, in its credentialing and privileging process for a physician who provides medical services to the patients at the hospital only through telemedicine from a distant-site hospital or distant-site telemedicine entity, a hospital may rely on the credentialing and privileging decisions made for the physician by the distant-site hospital or distant-site telemedicine entity as authorized under 42 C.F.R. Part 482, if:
The physician who provides medical services through telemedicine holds a license to practice medicine in the State under Health Occupations Article, Title 14, Annotated Code of Maryland; and
The credentialing and privileging decisions with respect to the physician who provides medical services through telemedicine are:
Approved by the medical staff of the hospital; and
Recommended by the medical staff of the hospital to the hospital’s governing body.
Request for Documentation by Department. On request from the Department, a hospital shall provide documentation that before:
Appointment or employment of a physician or granting delineated privileges, the hospital has complied with the requirements of this regulation; and
Reappointment or renewing of employment or specific privileges, the hospital has complied with the requirements of this regulation.
Penalties. If a hospital fails to have in effect a credentialing process in accordance with these regulations, the Secretary may impose upon the hospital the following penalties:
Delicensure of the hospital; or
A fine of $500 for each day that the hospital is in violation of these regulations.

para.text: The PCP shall include in its provider agreement a clause that the PCP will hold harmless both the Department and its enrollees from any liability for payment for the legal obligations of the PCP.
The PCP shall give the Department access to the PCP's financial records at reasonable intervals in order to monitor the PCP's financial solvency.
The PCP shall file annual audited financial statements with the Department. A PCP in operation for less than 1 year shall file an interim statement.
The provider agreement shall provide for the continuation of benefits to all enrollees for the duration of the contract period for which payment has been made.
The PCP shall have a financially sound operation as demonstrated by:
A positive net worth; and
Sufficient cash and adequate liquidity, as determined by the Department, to meet the PCP's obligations as they become due.

The PCP shall provide full and complete information as to the identity of each person or corporation with an ownership or control interest in the PCP and of any subcontractor in which the PCP has a 5 percent or more ownership interest.

PCPs that are at risk for services that the PCPs do not directly provide shall deposit an amount equal to the portion of 2 months' capitation payments relating to the services in a reserve account in trust. The agreement establishing the reserve account, which is subject to the Department's approval, may provide that the Department is the sole beneficiary of the trust, and that the trust funds may be disbursed only at the request of the Department to prevent or postpone the PCP's insolvency.
Minimum Insurance Coverage.

The PCP shall provide written evidence to the Department on an annual basis that it has sufficient insurance issued by an insurer authorized by the Maryland Insurance Administrator to engage in the insurance business to protect its financial viability and its ability to carry out its contractual obligations.
The coverage shall include at a minimum:
Malpractice coverage for all professional and related employees of the provider as well as for the organization itself;
Bonding of all employees and officers who have any responsibility for the accounting and financial management activities of the provider; and
Workers' compensation, fire, theft, casualty, and other coverage as required by State and local laws.

PCPs which are governmental units or are funded by the Behavioral Health Administration or the Developmental Disabilities Administration of the Department and subject to the oversight of one of these agencies are exempt from the requirements of this regulation.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Applicant” means an individual who has applied for presumptive eligibility at a participating qualified correctional facility.
“Application” means the presumptive eligibility application.
“Correctional facility” has the meaning stated in Correctional Services Article, §1-101, Annotated Code of Maryland, and includes:
Correctional facilities under the direction of the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services; and
Local correctional facilities within the State’s 24 local jurisdictions.

“Department” means the Department of Health, which is the single State agency designated to administer the Maryland Medical Assistance Program pursuant to Title XIX of the Social Security Act, 42 U.S.C. §1396 et seq.
“Determination” means a decision regarding an applicant’s presumptive eligibility.
“Federal poverty level” means the poverty guidelines updated periodically in the Federal Register by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services under the authority of 42 U.S.C. §9902(2).
“Former foster care” means an individual who:
Is younger than 26 years old;
Is not eligible and enrolled for coverage under a mandatory Medical Assistance group other than childless adult; and
Was formerly in a Maryland out-of-home placement, including categorical Medical Assistance:
On attaining age 18 and leaving out-of-home placement; or
On attaining age 19—21 during extended out-of-home placement under COMAR 07.02.11.04B.

“Incarcerated inmate” means an individual who resides in a public institution involuntarily as a result of being accused or found guilty of a criminal offense, including the duration of time in which the individual is involuntarily residing in the public institution in a preadjudication or pretrial status.
“Income” means property or a service received by an individual in cash or in-kind that can be applied directly, or by sale or conversion, to meet basic needs for food, shelter, and medical expenses.

“Medical Assistance” means the program administered by the State under Title XIX of the Social Security Act, which provides comprehensive medical and other health-related care for eligible individuals.
“Medicare” means the medical insurance program administered by the federal government under Title XVIII of the Social Security Act, 42, §U.S.C. 1395 et seq.

“Memorandum of understanding” means the agreement by which the correctional facility has contracted with the Department and been deemed a qualified entity eligible to make presumptive eligibility decisions.
“Presumptive eligibility” means temporary eligibility for Medical Assistance as determined by an eligibility worker at a participating qualified correctional facility in accordance with this chapter.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.

“Born” means the complete expulsion or extraction of a product of human conception from its mother, regardless of the period of gestation, if, after the expulsion or extraction, it breathes or shows any other evidence of life, such as heartbeat, pulsation of the umbilical cord, or definite movement of voluntary muscle, whether or not the umbilical cord is cut or the placenta is attached.
“Cord blood sampling” means the taking of a sample of a newborn infant's blood from the infant's umbilical cord.
“Footprinting” means the taking of an ink or other type of impression of the skin patterns located on the sole of the newborn infant's foot.
“Institution” means:
A hospital or related institution as defined in Health-General Article, §19-301, Annotated Code of Maryland;
A freestanding birthing center as defined in Health-General Article, §19-1101, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Any other facility that delivers an infant from its mother.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health or the Secretary's designee.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Alternative birthing site” means any site of birth other than a hospital.

“Appropriate services” mean any necessary audiological, medical, developmental, speech-language, sign language, psychosocial, educational, or intervention services required in the follow-up of newborns and infants who have or are at risk for developing a permanent hearing status that affects speech-language skills.
“At risk for developing a permanent hearing status that affects speech-language skills” means an infant identified as having any risk factor listed in Regulation .04 of this chapter.
“Audiological assessment” means testing to determine status of the auditory mechanism.
“Audiologist” means an individual licensed to practice audiology by the Maryland Board of Examiners for Audiologists, Hearing Aid Dispensers, and Speech-Language Pathologists.
“Birthing hospital” means a medical facility licensed by the Department which provides obstetrical services.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.
“Department database” means the computer data management system used to gather and maintain program data.

“Early Hearing Detection and Intervention Advisory Council (Advisory Council)” means the 12-member advisory council appointed by the Secretary to provide advice and consultation to the Department concerning the Early Hearing Detection and Intervention Program.

“Early intervention system” means the total effort in the State, for children from birth to 5 years of age, to meet the needs of eligible children and the children’s families, including the efforts of the Maryland Early Hearing Detection and Intervention Program.
“Hearing screening” means behavioral, electrophysiological, or other procedures used to identify newborns and infants in need of diagnostic audiological assessment.
“Hearing status” means the state of an individual’s ability to perceive sound, based on audiological assessment.
“Infant” means a Maryland resident who is younger than 1 year old.

“Infant with a permanent change in hearing status that affects speech-language skills” means an infant who has a status that is an atypical function of the auditory system or any nontransient hearing status of any type or degree which is sufficient to interfere with the acquisition and development of speech-language skills with or without the use of sound amplification.

“Local education agency” means a public board of education and public school system legally constituted to administer and direct education and programs of education in the public elementary or secondary schools, including early childhood from birth through the end of high school or their equivalent, in Baltimore City and the 23 counties in Maryland.
“Local health department” means the official public health agency of the local political subdivision affiliated with the Department.

“Maryland Infants and Toddlers Program” means the program within the Maryland State Department of Education’s Division of Special Education/Early Intervention Services that provides early intervention services for children under Part C of the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act.
“Newborn” means a child up to 29 days old who is born in the State.
“Parent” means a biological or adoptive parent, a legal guardian, another individual responsible for a child’s welfare, or a person appointed as the surrogate parent as defined in COMAR 13A.05.01.

“Primary care provider” means a licensed practitioner who is the primary coordinator of care for the individual, whose responsibility it is to provide accessible, continuous, comprehensive, and coordinated health care services.

“Program” means the Early Hearing Detection and Intervention Program established by the Secretary that provides for the universal screening of newborns and early identification and follow-up of newborns and infants who have, or are at risk for developing, a permanent hearing status that affects speech-language skills.

“Referral” means directing the newborn or infant at risk for developing a permanent hearing status that affects speech-language skills and the newborn or infant’s parent or guardian to a professional for diagnostic audiological assessment or other appropriate services, or both.
“Risk factors” means indicators that are associated with permanent congenital, delayed-onset, or progression to a hearing status that affects speech-language skills in childhood.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health or the Secretary’s designee.
“Telephone resource line” means toll-free telephone access to the Program inside Maryland through live voice or recorded message that is compatible with telecommunication relay services.

para.text: In this chapter, the following terms have the meanings indicated.
Terms Defined.
“Administration” means the Developmental Disabilities Administration (DDA).
“Alternative living unit (ALU)” means a residence that:
Provides residential services for individuals who, because of developmental disability, require specialized living arrangements;
Admits not more than three individuals; and
Provides 10 or more hours of supervision per unit, per week.
“Applicant” means the individual who files an application for services with the DDA.
“Application for services” means the instrument:
Used to make preliminary determination of eligibility for DDA services, the nature of the disability, the services required, and how these services are to be provided;
Providing the basis for preliminary determination of service priority.
“Appropriate evaluation” means the assessment of an individual using accepted professional standards to document the presence of a:
Developmental disability as defined in Health-General Article, §7-101, Annotated Code of Maryland; or
Disability that qualifies the individual for individual support services only as defined in Health-General Article, §7-403(c), Annotated Code of Maryland.

“Caregiver” means the person who is primarily responsible for the ongoing care of the individual who is applying for services from the DDA. If the applicant is the caregiver, this term is applicable to the applicant.

“Community Supported Living Arrangements Services (CSLA)” means one or more of the following services to assist an individual with developmental disability or an individual eligible for support services only in those nonvocational activities necessary to enable that individual to live in the individual's own home, apartment, family home, or rental unit, with not more than two other recipients of these services:
Personal assistance;
Supports that enhance the individual's opportunities for community participation and to exercise choice and control over the individual's own life;
Community integration training services necessary to assist the individual in achieving and maintaining increased integration, interdependence, and productivity;
24-hour emergency assistance;
Assistive technology;
Adaptive equipment;
Case management services;
Environmental modification;
Respite care; and
Other services as approved by the Secretary or the Secretary's designee.
“Department” means the Maryland Department of Health.

“Developmental Disabilities Administration access unit” (“DDA access unit” or “unit”) means the component which is under contract with the DDA to conduct the preliminary assessment of eligibility and priority for services based on the information provided on the application.
“Developmental disability” means a severe, chronic disability of an individual that:
Is attributable to a physical or mental impairment other than the sole diagnosis of mental illness, or to a combination of mental and physical impairments;
Is manifested before the individual becomes 22 years old;
Is likely to continue indefinitely;
Results in an inability to live independently without external support or continuing and regular assistance; and
Reflects the need for a combination and sequence of special, interdisciplinary, or generic care, treatment, or other services that are individually planned and coordinated for the individual.
“Director” means the Director of the Developmental Disabilities Administration.
“Eligibility for support services only” means an individual shall have a severe chronic disability that is:
Attributable to a physical or mental impairment other than the sole diagnosis of mental illness, or to a combination of mental and physical impairments; and
Likely to continue indefinitely.
“Family support services” means a program designed to enable a family to provide for the needs of a child with developmental disability living in the home. Family support services include:
Individual and family counseling;
Personal care;
Day care;
Specialized equipment;
Health services;
Respite care;
Housing adaptations;
Transportation; and
Other necessary services.
“Grabau class” means those individuals:
Who are confined in a SRC;
Whose level of intellectual and personal skills is sufficient to allow them to function outside the SRC;
Who have been characterized by the SRC as having maladaptive behavior or emotional disturbance of sufficient severity as to require their continued placement with the SRC; and
Who have been confirmed as members of the class through an appropriate evaluation and staffing process designated by the DDA.

“Home and Community Based Waiver (waiver)” means the document and any amendments to it submitted by the single State agency for Title XIX and approved by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, which authorizes the waiver of statutory requirements limiting coverage for home and community based services under the Medicaid State Plan.
“Individual” means the person for whom an application for services has been filed with the DDA.
“Individual family care home (IFC)” means a private single family residence licensed by the Department under COMAR 10.22.08 which:
Under supervision, provides a home for individuals with developmental disability in a family atmosphere; and
Provides habilitation services for one to three individuals who are not related to the caregiver.
Individual Support Services.
“Individual support services” means an array of services that are designed to increase or maintain an individual's ability to live alone or in a family setting.
“Individual support services” includes:
In-home assistance with meals and personal care;
Counseling;
Physical, occupational, or other therapies;
Architectural modification; and
Any other services that the Administration considers appropriate to meet the individual's needs.
“Knott class” means individuals with intellectual disabilities who were or are deemed inappropriately placed within the Department’s operated behavioral health facilities.
“Local review committee (LRC)” means the committee established by the regional director to review the service needs of eligible persons and make recommendations to the regional director.
“Low intensity support services” means support services in accordance with COMAR 10.22.06. Services qualifying for this category are:
Designed to be one-time only;
Low cost with a cap of $3,000 per individual per year; or
Approved by the regional office only if the cost of the services exceeds $3,000 or the services are needed beyond 1 calendar year.

“Personal contact” means a discussion of circumstances with the applicant, caregiver, or other proponent, when one exists, with a representative of the DDA, who has knowledge in the disability or has access to the assistance of someone with knowledge in the disability, for the purpose of gathering information relevant to the determination of eligibility, service needs, and priorities.

“Preliminary determination” means the outcome of the review of information supplied by or for the individual applying for DDA-funded services to produce an initial assessment of eligibility, service needs, and priorities.
“Program” means a prescribed set of services and activities designed to achieve specific objectives related to the needs of the individuals served.
“Proponent” means any person or agency who has a legitimate interest in the welfare of the individual applying for DDA services or being served in a DDA-licensed, operated, or funded program.
“Provider” means a licensed agency which provides services through an appropriate agreement with the Department.
“Regional director” means the director's designee who is charged with the management of the service delivery system in a specific geographic area of the State.
“Resident” means an individual who:
Demonstrates that that individual is living in the State voluntarily with an intent to remain on a permanent basis, including children with parents or guardians who reside out of the State;
Resides out-of-State but whose parents or guardians are residents of Maryland; or
Is a migrant worker and, while in the State, needs medical care and is not receiving assistance from any other state or political jurisdiction.
“Secretary” means the Secretary of Health.
“Secretary's designee” means the Director of the Developmental Disabilities Administration.

“Service coordinator” means a professional who is assigned the responsibility for assisting the consumer in accessing the service delivery system more efficiently and effectively. This assistance is effected through planning, monitoring, and coordinating the medical, social, habilitative, and vocational services necessary to meet the identified needs of the consumer, as agreed upon and specified in the consumer's individualized service plan.
“State residential center (SRC)” means a place that:
Is owned and operated by the State;
Provides residential services for individuals with intellectual disabilities and who, because of intellectual disabilities, require specialized living arrangements; and
Admits nine or more individuals with intellectual disabilities.
“Waiting list” means, for the purpose of this chapter, those individuals considered eligible for services and who have not received those services under the auspices of these regulations.

section.heading: Eligibility Criteria and Preliminary Determination Process.

para.text: Eligibility Criteria. To be eligible for services funded by the DDA as an individual with developmental disability, that individual shall:
Be a resident of Maryland; and
Have an evaluation that finds that a developmental disability is present.
Preliminary Determination Process.

The DDA access unit shall complete the preliminary determination and send the applicant, caregiver, or other proponent, when one exists, a letter containing the results within 7 calendar days of receipt of the application as indicated in Regulation .04C(2),of this chapter.
The preliminary determination consists of a review of all information supplied by or for the individual to determine the presence of an apparent disability which meets the eligibility requirements.
The DDA access unit shall send a letter to the applicant, caregiver, or other proponent, when one exists, which contains the following information:
The DDA access unit has received the application;

The individual appears to have a developmental disability, or does not appear to have a developmental disability, but may be eligible for individual support services in accordance with Health-General Article, §7-403(c), Annotated Code of Maryland;

The applicant, caregiver, or other proponent, when one exists, shall receive a personal contact in accordance with Regulation .03B(21) of this chapter, or the individual is not eligible for services funded by the DDA and no further action shall be taken by the DDA, absent an appeal of this determination;
The individual may be eligible for services from the Behavioral Health Administration; and
The individual has the right to an informal hearing to appeal the decision in accordance with COMAR 10.22.16.

section.heading: Eligibility Determination Process — Determination of Service Priority Category and Description of Categories.

para.text: Determination of Service Priority Category.

The regional director shall base the recommendation regarding service priority on the determination that the individual meets the criteria for one or more of the following categories, which are listed in the order of priority in which applicants shall receive services:
Category I — Crisis Resolution;
Category II — Crisis Prevention; and
Category III — Current Request.
Individuals shall be served according to their priority ranking as follows:
Individuals in Category I are to be served before those in Category II; and
Individuals in Category II are to be served before those in Category III.
Service delivery is dependent upon levels of funding allocated for the fiscal year for the following discrete categories:
Category IV — Transitioning Youth;
Category V — Knott Class Member;
Category VI — Inappropriate Institutionalization; and
Category VII — Innovation or Demonstration Projects.

Individuals may be in more than one priority category at the same time. For example, an individual in the transitioning youth priority category for a day program may also be in the crisis prevention priority category for a residential program if the individual is graduating or leaving a secondary school program and has no place to live after that program ends.
Description of Categories.
Category I — Crisis Resolution.
To qualify for this category, the applicant shall meet one or more of the following criteria. The applicant shall be:
Homeless or living in temporary housing with clear time-limited ability to continue to live in this setting with no viable non-DDA-funded alternative;
At serious risk of physical harm in the current environment;
At serious risk of causing physical harm to others in the current environment;
In danger of losing DDA-funded residential services because of a lack of current day services;
One who has lost DDA-funded day services; or
Living with a caregiver who is unable to provide adequate care due to the caregiver's impaired health, which may place the applicant at risk of serious physical harm.
To qualify for Category I under §B(1)(a)(ii), of this regulation, evidence such as the following shall be necessary. The applicant:
Has recently received severe injuries due to the behavior of others in the home or community;
Has recently been the victim of sexual abuse;
Has been neglected to the extent that the individual is at serious risk of sustaining injuries which are life threatening or which substantially impair functioning;
Engages in self-injurious behavior which puts the individual at serious risk of sustaining injuries which are life threatening or which substantially impair functioning; or
Is at serious risk of sustaining injuries which are life threatening or which substantially impair functioning due to the physical surroundings.
If the applicant qualifies for Category I under §B(1)(a)(v), of this regulation, the individual shall qualify for day services only.
If the applicant is living in a situation where the applicant is the caregiver, §B(1)(a)(vi), of this regulation, shall apply to the applicant.
Individuals who qualify for services under this category shall, at a minimum, remain in this category until they have been provided with those services required to resolve the situation.
Individuals become eligible to receive services as the need occurs.
Category II — Crisis Prevention.
To qualify for this priority category, the applicant:
Shall have been determined by the DDA to have an urgent need for services;
May not qualify for services based on the criteria for Category I; and
Shall be at substantial risk for meeting one or more of the criteria in §B(1)(a) of this regulation, within 1 year, or have a caregiver who is 65 years old or more.
Individuals who qualify for services under this category shall, at a minimum, remain in this category until they have been provided with those services required to resolve the situation.

Individuals become eligible to receive services from the date of approval of priority status, except when eligibility is determined by the age of the caregiver. In this case, priority is determined by the caregiver's date of birth so that individuals with caregivers born at an earlier date have priority over individuals with caregivers born at a later date.
Category III — Current Request.
To qualify for this priority category, the applicant shall indicate at least a current need for services.
Prioritization of Services.
Applicants shall be prioritized for services based on the number of fiscal years they have been on the waiting list, except as provided for in §B(3)(b)(iv) of this regulation.
Applicants on the waiting list for the longest period of time shall receive services before those who have been on the list for fewer years, except as provided for in §B(3)(b)(iv) of this regulation.

Applicants whose applications are received by the DDA access unit within a given fiscal year shall be ranked by the fiscal year of application, and the month and day of birth. Those applicants born at the beginning of the fiscal year have priority over those born later in the year.
For day programs only, the period of time that shall be counted toward prioritizing an individual shall begin with the year of departure from school or the year of application, whichever is later.
Category IV — Transitioning Youth.
To qualify for funding for services in this priority category the applicant shall be eligible:

For DDA-funded services in this category from the individual's 21st to the individual's 22nd birthday. If the date of graduation is after the individual's 21st birthday, the individual shall continue to be eligible for 1 year after the date of graduation.
To receive day services only.
Individuals in this priority category shall also be in one or more of the other priority categories.
Individuals become eligible to receive services from the date of approval of priority status.
Category V — Knott Class.
To qualify for this priority category, the applicant shall have:
An intellectual disability; and
Been determined inappropriately retained in a Department operated behavioral health facility as set forth in Knott vs. Hughes Civil Action No. Y-80-2832 (Fed. Dist. Ct. Md).
Individuals become eligible to receive services based on the best clinical judgment of the professionals involved based on availability of allocated resources.
Category VI — Inappropriate Institutionalization.
To qualify for this priority category, the applicant shall be a:
Resident in a nursing facility and not meet the criteria for admission or retention for that facility;

Resident of an intermediate care facility for individuals with intellectual disabilities or persons with related conditions (ICF/IID) and not meet the criteria for admission or retention for that facility as determined by the hearing examiner;
Resident in a chronic hospital who does not meet the criteria for admission or retention for that facility;

Resident in a behavioral health facility operated by the Department who has a developmental disability but is not an individual with an intellectual disability and who does not meet the criteria for admission or retention in a State psychiatric hospital and whose primary need is not for a mental health service.
An individual in this priority category shall also be in another priority category.
Funding may be allocated for one or more of the above groups in §B(6)(a)(i)—(iv) of this regulation.
Individuals become eligible to receive services based on the best clinical judgment of the professionals involved based on availability of allocated resources.
Category VII — Innovation or Demonstration Projects.

To identify those individuals for whom this priority category is appropriate, the Administration, after consultation with interested parties, shall publish a request for proposal (RFP) for innovation or demonstration projects.
To qualify for this priority category, an individual shall be identified in a proposal submitted to the Administration which is considered appropriate for funding by the Administration.
An individual served in this priority category may continue to receive services, to the extent that funds are allocated, after the termination of the innovation or demonstration period.
An individual in this priority category may also be in another priority category.
Eligible applicants may receive family support services and low intensity support services on a first-come, first-served basis, regardless of service priority determination.

para.text: In determining whether to impose an administrative fine on a licensee for any violation under Transportation Article, Title 15, Annotated Code of Maryland, the Administration shall consider:
The seriousness of the violation;
The deleterious effect of the violation on others;
Any acts of good faith on the part of the licensee;
The licensee's history of previous violations; and
Previous counseling or reprimands on the issues under consideration;
Failure to submit title and registration data, fees, and taxes may result in a standard fine which is assessed at a rate of:
For a tag and title service that submits transaction information later than required by the contract between the tag and title service and the Administration, the standard fine shall be:
For the first late day after the requirement to submit by contract, $30; and
Increased by $1 per day thereafter.
For a dealership that submits transaction information later than 30 days after the transaction date, the standard fine shall be:
For the first day after 30 days, $30; and
Increased by $1 per day thereafter.

Fines levied in §B of this regulation are the standard fines and can be adjusted as determined by the Administration based on circumstance. Nothing in this section limits additional administrative action as determined by the Administration including license suspension and revocation.
